trans-unit.source: Drought EAP for southern Madagascar: Hazard and Vulnerability analysis
trans-unit.target: Drought EAP for southern Madagascar: Hazard and Vulnerability analysis

trans-unit.source: :::{topic} Context For the Drought Early Action Protocol (EAP) by the Malagasy Red Cross (CRM), we developed a hazard and vulnerability analysis. The result ware reproducible maps showing a composite drought vulnerability combining hazard recurrence (rainfall deficits) and documented humanitarian impacts (IPC phase 3 or above). The two indicators are combined and classified into three levels of drought vulnerability (High/Medium/Low). :::
trans-unit.target: :::{topic} Context For the Drought Early Action Protocol (EAP) by the Malagasy Red Cross (CRM), we developed a hazard and vulnerability analysis. The result ware reproducible maps showing a composite drought vulnerability combining hazard recurrence (rainfall deficits) and documented humanitarian impacts (IPC phase 3 or above). The two indicators are combined and classified into three levels of drought vulnerability (High/Medium/Low). :::

trans-unit.source: :::{card} __QGIS Project__ ^^^ The QGIS project and the datasets used in this project can be found here: __[MDG_DROUGHT_EAP.qgz]()__
trans-unit.target: :::{card} __QGIS Project__ ^^^ The QGIS project and the datasets used in this project can be found here: __[MDG_DROUGHT_EAP.qgz]()__

trans-unit.source: Note: Copyrighted datasets (CHIRPS and IPC) must be downloaded from the respective publishers or Google Earth Engine.
trans-unit.target: Note: Copyrighted datasets (CHIRPS and IPC) must be downloaded from the respective publishers or Google Earth Engine.

trans-unit.source: Part 1: Historical rainfall deficit recurrence map (CHIRPS-based, 2000–2024) aggregated at district level
trans-unit.target: Part 1: Historical rainfall deficit recurrence map (CHIRPS-based, 2000–2024) aggregated at district level

trans-unit.source: __Objective:__
trans-unit.target: __Objective:__

trans-unit.source: Show spatial recurrence of significant rainfall deficits in southern Madagascar over a long reference period (ideally 2000–2024). Suggested approach (open to your advice):
trans-unit.target: Show spatial recurrence of significant rainfall deficits in southern Madagascar over a long reference period (ideally 2000–2024). Suggested approach (open to your advice):

trans-unit.source: Use CHIRPS monthly or seasonal data.
trans-unit.target: Use CHIRPS monthly or seasonal data.

trans-unit.source: Calculate anomalies relative to long-term averages.
trans-unit.target: Calculate anomalies relative to long-term averages.

trans-unit.source: Define a threshold for severe deficit (e.g. ≤ -30% or ≤ -40%).
trans-unit.target: Define a threshold for severe deficit (e.g. ≤ -30% or ≤ -40%).

trans-unit.source: Count frequency of deficit events over the reference period.
trans-unit.target: Count frequency of deficit events over the reference period.

trans-unit.source: Aggregate at district level (ADM2).
trans-unit.target: Aggregate at district level (ADM2).

trans-unit.source: __Output:__
trans-unit.target: __Output:__

trans-unit.source: District-level choropleth (High / Medium / Low recurrence).
trans-unit.target: District-level choropleth (High / Medium / Low recurrence).

trans-unit.source: Clear, simple methodological note explaining threshold and aggregation.
trans-unit.target: Clear, simple methodological note explaining threshold and aggregation.

trans-unit.source: __Questions:__
trans-unit.target: __Questions:__

trans-unit.source: CHIRPS monthly or seasonal data: How to define seasons? -> look at rain seasons in mdg or simply DJF, MAM
trans-unit.target: CHIRPS monthly or seasonal data: How to define seasons? -> look at rain seasons in mdg or simply DJF, MAM

trans-unit.source: Should I explain my choice for `Daily_RNL` instead of `Daily_SAT`?
trans-unit.target: Should I explain my choice for `Daily_RNL` instead of `Daily_SAT`?

trans-unit.source: Is the question: *How widespread are rainfall deficits in the districts?* OR *How widespread are rainfall deficits where livelihoods depend on rainfall?*
trans-unit.target: Is the question: *How widespread are rainfall deficits in the districts?* OR *How widespread are rainfall deficits where livelihoods depend on rainfall?*

trans-unit.source: I calculate the meterological mean per month over the last 25 years. and calculated the anomalies per month. I then put a threshold of 30% deficit to indicate severe deficit and calculated how many months are above that threshold (= recurrence of deficit).
trans-unit.target: I calculate the meterological mean per month over the last 25 years. and calculated the anomalies per month. I then put a threshold of 30% deficit to indicate severe deficit and calculated how many months are above that threshold (= recurrence of deficit).

trans-unit.source: __Problem:__ Does that indicate drought or hydrological stress or **unstable monthly rainfall**?
trans-unit.target: __Problem:__ Does that indicate drought or hydrological stress or **unstable monthly rainfall**?

trans-unit.source: Also: In very dry months (e.g. July–September in the south):
trans-unit.target: Also: In very dry months (e.g. July–September in the south):

trans-unit.source: monthly mean rainfall may be very small
trans-unit.target: monthly mean rainfall may be very small

trans-unit.source: A small absolute deviation can exceed −30%
trans-unit.target: A small absolute deviation can exceed −30%

trans-unit.source: This can inflate recurrence and create "deficits that have no impact relevance
trans-unit.target: This can inflate recurrence and create "deficits that have no impact relevance

trans-unit.source: Workflow: Mapping hazard recurrence
trans-unit.target: Workflow: Mapping hazard recurrence

trans-unit.source: Use GEE to aggregate the daily CHIRPS v3 to monthly totals for the time period 2000 to 2024.
trans-unit.target: Use GEE to aggregate the daily CHIRPS v3 to monthly totals for the time period 2000 to 2024.

trans-unit.source: Calculate the monthly climatology (mean per month across all years).
trans-unit.target: Calculate the monthly climatology (mean per month across all years).

trans-unit.source: Compute the percentage anomaly per month %anom=100×(P−Pˉm​)/Pˉm​ (per cell)
trans-unit.target: Compute the percentage anomaly per month %anom=100×(P−Pˉm​)/Pˉm​ (per cell)

trans-unit.source: Flag severe deficit months (e.g. <= -30%)
trans-unit.target: Flag severe deficit months (e.g. <= -30%)

trans-unit.source: Count how often each pixel is in deficit (frequency or count) = recurrence value
trans-unit.target: Count how often each pixel is in deficit (frequency or count) = recurrence value

trans-unit.source: Aggregate to admin level:
trans-unit.target: Aggregate to admin level:

trans-unit.source: __Options:__
trans-unit.target: __Options:__

trans-unit.source: A: Mean pixel recurrence (can also be normalised by dividing with the total number of months -> %)
trans-unit.target: A: Mean pixel recurrence (can also be normalised by dividing with the total number of months -> %)

trans-unit.source: B: Percent of area affected (need to define a threshhold of recurring deficit months, e.g., more than 30 deficit month in time period)
trans-unit.target: B: Percent of area affected (need to define a threshhold of recurring deficit months, e.g., more than 30 deficit month in time period)

trans-unit.source: "65% of district had more than 30 deficit months".
trans-unit.target: "65% of district had more than 30 deficit months".

trans-unit.source: Monthly rainfall totals were derived from CHIRP v3 daily data hosted on Google Earth Engine. Monthly anomalies were calculated relative to the 2000–2024 climatological mean for each calendar month. Rainfall deficit events were defined as months with precipitation ≤30% (moderate) or ≤40% (severe) below the long-term mean (per month?). The frequency of deficit events was aggregated at district (ADM2) level to characterize historical recurrence.
trans-unit.target: Monthly rainfall totals were derived from CHIRP v3 daily data hosted on Google Earth Engine. Monthly anomalies were calculated relative to the 2000–2024 climatological mean for each calendar month. Rainfall deficit events were defined as months with precipitation ≤30% (moderate) or ≤40% (severe) below the long-term mean (per month?). The frequency of deficit events was aggregated at district (ADM2) level to characterize historical recurrence.

trans-unit.source: Rainfall deficit recurrence was defined as the number of months between 2000 and 2024 in which monthly rainfall fell below the deficit threshold. Recurrence was first calculated at the pixel level and then spatially averaged within administrative districts to characterize the typical frequency of deficit conditions across each district.
trans-unit.target: Rainfall deficit recurrence was defined as the number of months between 2000 and 2024 in which monthly rainfall fell below the deficit threshold. Recurrence was first calculated at the pixel level and then spatially averaged within administrative districts to characterize the typical frequency of deficit conditions across each district.

trans-unit.source: :::{card} __Calculated indicators__ ^^^ | Level | Variable | Interpretation | | ----- | ------------------ | -------------------------------------- | | Pixel | (D_t(x)) | Deficit this month? (0/1) | | Pixel | (R(x)) | # of deficit months at that location | | ADM2 | mean (R(x)) | Typical deficit recurrence in district | | ADM2 | % area ≥ threshold | Spatial extent of recurrent deficits | :::
trans-unit.target: :::{card} __Calculated indicators__ ^^^ | Level | Variable | Interpretation | | ----- | ------------------ | -------------------------------------- | | Pixel | (D_t(x)) | Deficit this month? (0/1) | | Pixel | (R(x)) | # of deficit months at that location | | ADM2 | mean (R(x)) | Typical deficit recurrence in district | | ADM2 | % area ≥ threshold | Spatial extent of recurrent deficits | :::

trans-unit.source: We have calculated two indicators:
trans-unit.target: We have calculated two indicators:

trans-unit.source: Mean recurrence per adm2
trans-unit.target: Mean recurrence per adm2

trans-unit.source: Percentage of area above threshold Now we need to select the thresholds for both indicators:
trans-unit.target: Percentage of area above threshold Now we need to select the thresholds for both indicators:

trans-unit.source: Select thresholds for the choropleth map:
trans-unit.target: Select thresholds for the choropleth map:

trans-unit.source: quantiles: 3 classes (high/medium/low)
trans-unit.target: quantiles: 3 classes (high/medium/low)

trans-unit.source: absolute thresholds:
trans-unit.target: absolute thresholds:

trans-unit.source: Low: < 10% of months in deficit
trans-unit.target: Low: < 10% of months in deficit

trans-unit.source: Medium: 10-20% of months in deficit
trans-unit.target: Medium: 10-20% of months in deficit

trans-unit.source: High: > 20% of months in deficit
trans-unit.target: High: > 20% of months in deficit

trans-unit.source: standard deviation? :::
trans-unit.target: standard deviation? :::

trans-unit.source: __define K__
trans-unit.target: __define K__

trans-unit.source: define classes for percentages:
trans-unit.target: define classes for percentages:

trans-unit.source: ___Data-distribution:__
trans-unit.target: ___Data-distribution:__

trans-unit.source: tertiles
trans-unit.target: tertiles

trans-unit.source: __Absolute exposure:__
trans-unit.target: __Absolute exposure:__

trans-unit.source: e.g., less than 10 % -> localised/patchy
trans-unit.target: e.g., less than 10 % -> localised/patchy

trans-unit.source: 10-30% -> substantial exposure
trans-unit.target: 10-30% -> substantial exposure

trans-unit.source: more than 30% -> widespread exposure
trans-unit.target: more than 30% -> widespread exposure

trans-unit.source: __sensitivity-informed__
trans-unit.target: __sensitivity-informed__

trans-unit.source: examine how % area affected behaves for different thresholds ::: ::::
trans-unit.target: examine how % area affected behaves for different thresholds ::: ::::

trans-unit.source: __Output:__
trans-unit.target: __Output:__

trans-unit.source: a district layer with fields:
trans-unit.target: a district layer with fields:

trans-unit.source: `def_count`: number of deficit months, 2000-2024
trans-unit.target: `def_count`: number of deficit months, 2000-2024

trans-unit.source: `def_freq`: def count / number of months
trans-unit.target: `def_freq`: def count / number of months

trans-unit.source: maybe: counts by season (DJF, MAM, etc.)
trans-unit.target: maybe: counts by season (DJF, MAM, etc.)

trans-unit.source: Preliminary Map Results
trans-unit.target: Preliminary Map Results

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} GEE Script
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} GEE Script

trans-unit.source: Part 2: A recurrent IPC Phase 3+ exposure layer (if feasible)
trans-unit.target: Part 2: A recurrent IPC Phase 3+ exposure layer (if feasible)

trans-unit.source: __Objective:__ Complement meteorological layer with documented humanitarian impact evidence.
trans-unit.target: __Objective:__ Complement meteorological layer with documented humanitarian impact evidence.

trans-unit.source: __Possible options:__
trans-unit.target: __Possible options:__

trans-unit.source: Overlay IPC Phase 3+ outcomes (where data available)
trans-unit.target: Overlay IPC Phase 3+ outcomes (where data available)

trans-unit.source: Estimate recurrence of IPC 3+ at district level across recent years.
trans-unit.target: Estimate recurrence of IPC 3+ at district level across recent years.

trans-unit.source: If full GIS integration is complex, even a spatial proxy based on documented IPC rounds would already be extremely helpful.
trans-unit.target: If full GIS integration is complex, even a spatial proxy based on documented IPC rounds would already be extremely helpful.

trans-unit.source: __Questions:__
trans-unit.target: __Questions:__

trans-unit.source: What is the temporal range for recurrence at district level? IPC reports are published every few months iirc and I calculate the CHIRPS recurrence on a monthly basis. -> *reports are irregular in FEWSNET. I can replicate the workflow with ipcinfo data easily it just takes some time to download the reports manually.*
trans-unit.target: What is the temporal range for recurrence at district level? IPC reports are published every few months iirc and I calculate the CHIRPS recurrence on a monthly basis. -> *reports are irregular in FEWSNET. I can replicate the workflow with ipcinfo data easily it just takes some time to download the reports manually.*

trans-unit.source: how should I aggregate the IPC phases at district level if the IPC phases are not tied to admin boundaries. Area share also does not make sense because it does not show where people live. *-> solved during analysis. Geographic units orient themselves along adm2 boundaries and slivers were easy to detect due to their area size.*
trans-unit.target: how should I aggregate the IPC phases at district level if the IPC phases are not tied to admin boundaries. Area share also does not make sense because it does not show where people live. *-> solved during analysis. Geographic units orient themselves along adm2 boundaries and slivers were easy to detect due to their area size.*

trans-unit.source: To get the time resolution, I could aggregate the CHIRPS using % of months in deficit? *-> doesn't matter because they didn't ask for it.*
trans-unit.target: To get the time resolution, I could aggregate the CHIRPS using % of months in deficit? *-> doesn't matter because they didn't ask for it.*

trans-unit.source: ML1 oder ML2? -> __CS__
trans-unit.target: ML1 oder ML2? -> __CS__

trans-unit.source: In FEWSNET or via IPCinfo?
trans-unit.target: In FEWSNET or via IPCinfo?

trans-unit.source: __Step 1: Downloading IPC data from FEWSNET:__
trans-unit.target: __Step 1: Downloading IPC data from FEWSNET:__

trans-unit.source: List all the publications available through the API.
trans-unit.target: List all the publications available through the API.

trans-unit.source: I can also extract the CSV:
trans-unit.target: I can also extract the CSV:

trans-unit.source: FEWS NET’s spatial model is built around Geographic Units with stable IDs called FNIDs.
trans-unit.target: FEWS NET’s spatial model is built around Geographic Units with stable IDs called FNIDs.

trans-unit.source: Pulling csv of geogrpahic units (without geometry):
trans-unit.target: Pulling csv of geogrpahic units (without geometry):

trans-unit.source: Pulling geometries:
trans-unit.target: Pulling geometries:

trans-unit.source: :::{admonition} IPC data availability pre 2016 :class: note
trans-unit.target: :::{admonition} IPC data availability pre 2016 :class: note

trans-unit.source: 2016+ IPC CS data is internally consistent
trans-unit.target: 2016+ IPC CS data is internally consistent

trans-unit.source: pre-2016 is not directly comparable
trans-unit.target: pre-2016 is not directly comparable

trans-unit.source: Before ~2015–2016, FEWS NET and partners used:
trans-unit.target: Before ~2015–2016, FEWS NET and partners used:

trans-unit.source: FEWS NET food security classifications (pre-IPC standardization)
trans-unit.target: FEWS NET food security classifications (pre-IPC standardization)

trans-unit.source: IPC-compatible analyses in some countries/years
trans-unit.target: IPC-compatible analyses in some countries/years

trans-unit.source: Narrative reports with maps, not always machine-readable
trans-unit.target: Narrative reports with maps, not always machine-readable

trans-unit.source: __Bottom line:__ There's no IPC classifications pre 2016 i can use. Neither on FEWSNET nor IPCinfo. :::
trans-unit.target: __Bottom line:__ There's no IPC classifications pre 2016 i can use. Neither on FEWSNET nor IPCinfo. :::

trans-unit.source: Avoid ML1 as it is based on rainfall projections and it introduces circularity if we compare it to CHIRPS anomalies.
trans-unit.target: Avoid ML1 as it is based on rainfall projections and it introduces circularity if we compare it to CHIRPS anomalies.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Choosing between IPCinfo and FEWSNET IPCinfo publication cycles are published in their own 3 month cycles, and not attached to seasons.
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Choosing between IPCinfo and FEWSNET IPCinfo publication cycles are published in their own 3 month cycles, and not attached to seasons.

trans-unit.source: FEWSNET cycles are monthly, but in the early years also irregular.
trans-unit.target: FEWSNET cycles are monthly, but in the early years also irregular.

trans-unit.source: If you use ML1, you must label it as projected Crisis+ risk recurrence, not “observed impact recurrence.” :::
trans-unit.target: If you use ML1, you must label it as projected Crisis+ risk recurrence, not “observed impact recurrence.” :::

trans-unit.source: I will go with the FEWSNET CS data
trans-unit.target: I will go with the FEWSNET CS data

trans-unit.source: Should I use recurrence rate for ipc phase because reporting dates are irregular in FEWSNET.
trans-unit.target: Should I use recurrence rate for ipc phase because reporting dates are irregular in FEWSNET.

trans-unit.source: Calculating the IPC Phase
trans-unit.target: Calculating the IPC Phase

trans-unit.source: :::{note} Make sure the units are in meters when calculating area. `$area / 1e6` to get area in km². :::
trans-unit.target: :::{note} Make sure the units are in meters when calculating area. `$area / 1e6` to get area in km². :::

trans-unit.source: :::{note} The time series includes classifications with the IPC classification 2.1 and 3.0. Should be noted somewhere.
trans-unit.target: :::{note} The time series includes classifications with the IPC classification 2.1 and 3.0. Should be noted somewhere.

trans-unit.source: Merge IPC layers
trans-unit.target: Merge IPC layers

trans-unit.source: Reproject to WGS 84 Pseudo Mercator (EPSG:3857)
trans-unit.target: Reproject to WGS 84 Pseudo Mercator (EPSG:3857)

trans-unit.source: Reproject to Tananarive (Paris) / Laborde Grid (EPSG:29701) for metric units
trans-unit.target: Reproject to Tananarive (Paris) / Laborde Grid (EPSG:29701) for metric units

trans-unit.source: Intersect with adm2 boundaries (fields 2 copy: adm2 pcode).
trans-unit.target: Intersect with adm2 boundaries (fields 2 copy: adm2 pcode).

trans-unit.source: Dissolve per adm2 and report date -> only 1 IPC phase 3+ polygon per report date and adm2 polygon
trans-unit.target: Dissolve per adm2 and report date -> only 1 IPC phase 3+ polygon per report date and adm2 polygon

trans-unit.source: calculate area in km² (`$area / 1e6`).
trans-unit.target: calculate area in km² (`$area / 1e6`).

trans-unit.source: Delete sliver (< 2 km²)
trans-unit.target: Delete sliver (< 2 km²)

trans-unit.source: Satistics per categories (category: adm2_pcode, fields to calculate stats: adm2_pcode)
trans-unit.target: Satistics per categories (category: adm2_pcode, fields to calculate stats: adm2_pcode)

trans-unit.source: join per attribute value:
trans-unit.target: join per attribute value:

trans-unit.source: adm2 boundaries
trans-unit.target: adm2 boundaries

trans-unit.source: statistics per category
trans-unit.target: statistics per category

trans-unit.source: key value: adm2_pcode
trans-unit.target: key value: adm2_pcode

trans-unit.source: fields to copy: count
trans-unit.target: fields to copy: count

trans-unit.source: __Output:__ admin2 layer with count of ipc phase 3+
trans-unit.target: __Output:__ admin2 layer with count of ipc phase 3+

trans-unit.source: __Problem:__ Publication cycle is irregular on FEWSNET. CS was only published twice per year in the last few years. Does it make more sense to calculate IPC phase 3+ rate over the last publications?
trans-unit.target: __Problem:__ Publication cycle is irregular on FEWSNET. CS was only published twice per year in the last few years. Does it make more sense to calculate IPC phase 3+ rate over the last publications?
